key_insight: A simple feed-forward neural surrogate, designed to emulate the return-mapping update, can replace expensive nested fine-scale solves in FE2 plasticity while preserving the macroscopic response.
engineering_relevance: This makes multiscale plasticity simulation practical for standard finite-element workflows by cutting analysis time from hours to seconds and enabling UMAT deployment.
method: Generate training data from incremental homogenization/FFT-based meso-scale analyses.
Use a compact network that encodes material symmetries and mirrors the classical return-mapping structure.
Deploy the trained surrogate as a UMAT in a conventional finite-element solver.
evidence_signal: In the macroscopically isotropic 2D plane-stress case, the surrogate matched the reference FE2 response and achieved speed-ups up to 30,000×, with a sensitivity study on data density, increment size, and mesh refinement.
watch_item: The surrogate is only reliable within the sampled regime; performance can degrade for insufficient data, larger increments, or mesh/refinement settings outside the training distribution.
paper_title: Lightweight return-mapping surrogates for multiscale plasticity: a practical guide
